paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E123-0217 (Reapproved 0623) SPECIFICATION FOR HANDLER EQUIPMENT SPECIFIC EQUIPMENT MODEL (HSEM) This Standard was technically approved by the Information & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on December 21, 2022. Available at www.semiviews.org and www.semi.org in June 2023; originally published March 2003; previously published February 2017. 1 Purpose 1.1 This Standard establishes a Specific Equipment Model (SEM) for Handling equipment (HSEM). The SEM consists of equipment characteristics and behaviors that are applicable to this class of equipment. The intent of this Standard is to facilitate the integration of Handling equipment into an automated (semiconductor) factory. This Standard accomplishes this by defining an operational model for Handling equipment as viewed by a factory automation controller. This definition provides a standard host interface and equipment operational behavior. 2 Scope 2.1 The scope of this Standard is limited to the definition of Handling equipment behavior as perceived by a host. It defines the view of the equipment through the equipment communications interface. It does not define the internal operation of the equipment. It includes a specific processing state model as the basis for the behavior of all equipment of this class. 2.2 This Standard expands the handler equipment requirements and capabilities in the areas of the processing state model, collection event, alarm documentation, remote commands, variables item, and process program management. OTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It

I Communications - HSEM-compliant equipment shall support the High Speed Messaging Service (HSMS) Communication Standard sending SEMI ES messages over TCP/IP. The reason behind this requirement is the amount of data available for monitoring from this class of equipment. This Specification deals only with the behavior of the handler in communicating with the host. 3.2 Multi-Process-Site HSEM Implementations - This SEM makes some demands and assumptions about the Handler with multiple process-sites in a configuration. These requirements are as follows: 3.2.1 Handling equipment in a multiple process-site configuration (i.e., lead conditioning site, electrical test-site) provides identification and status information (refer to Variable Item) at both the site level and the subsite level. An example could be a handler with both a lead conditioning site and an electrical test-site, with the electrical test-site containing multiple subsites (i.e., test heads). 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines SEMI E30 - Specification for the Generic Model for Communications and Control of Manufacturing Equipment (GEM) OTICE: Unless otherwise indicated,

SEMI E123-0217 (Reapproved 0623) SEMI 2003, 2023 semr 6.2 State Model Requirements 6.2.1 The processing state models in this Standard are required for implementing an HSEM-compliant handler. A state model consists of the following state model diagram, state definitions, and state transition tables. 6.2.2 A state model represents the host's view of the handler, not necessarily the actual handler's internal operations. 6.2.3 All HSEM state model transitions shall be mapped sequentially into the actual equipment events that satisfy the requirements of those transitions. In certain implementations, the handler may enter a state and have already satisfied all of the conditions required by the HSEM state model for transition to another state. paragraphs.context: cleared. 6.4.10 PAUSED (PROCESS PAUSE Substate)-The PROCESS state has been suspended, and the handler is waiting for a command (RESUME, STOP, or ABORT). In this state, the operator may correct error conditions that do not affect the current Process Program selection. One of the possible corrective actions is for the operator to manually align the units being processed. 6.4.11 PAUSING (PROCESS PAUSE Substate) -The PROCESS state will be suspended at the completion of the current unit or next opportunity. The handler cannot transition to PAUSED state until the current unit is completed and the handler is in a 'safe state.' 6.4.12 AWAITING COMMAND (WORKING Substate) -Unit is moved to process-site (e.g., for electrical test, to insert into contactor). Actuator piston(s) is extended to the alignment test site(s) if applicable. If contactors must be 'closed' before processing can occur, then contactors are in the closed condition. In this state, handler has completed all loading and is awaiting the next command from Host to signal process complete. 6.4.13 CONTACTOR OPEN (WORKING Substate) - Unit process is complete and host issued a BREAK CONT ACT or RECO TACT command (e.g., Actuator piston(s) is retracted from the alignment test site(s), contactors are open). If RECONTACT command was issued, unit will return to alignment for a step and repeat. Otherwise Handler will remain in this state until a MAKE-CO TACT is received to resume processing. Commands such as STOP, ABORT and PAUSE are still valid and follow the respective required transitions. 6.4.14 PRELOADJNG (PROCESS Substate)

Commands such as STOP, ABORT and PAUSE are still valid and follow the respective required transitions. 6.4.14 PRELOADJNG (PROCESS Substate) - Handler has received a PRELOAD-UNITS command to begin preloading units to soak chambers or some equivalent process buffers. Units should not be transferred to process sites in this state. During this time, test equipment may also go through its internal setup process in parallel, such as testhead calibrations. If handler does not implement any process buffers, the remote command from Host must still be accepted and state transition followed, in which case handler will transition back to READY state immediately with PreloadComplete event sent to Host. 6.4.15 PROCESS (PROCESS Substate) -This state is the parent of those substates that refer to the preparation and execution of a process program. 6.4.16 PROCESS PAUSE (PAUSE Substate)-The handler is free of alarm conditions in the PAUSE state. 6.4.17 READY (PROCESS Substate) -The handler is ready to begin processing and is awaiting a START or PRELOAD-UNITS command from the operator or host. 6.4.18 SETTING UP (PROCESS Substate) -The handler is satisfying conditions so that processing can begin. This includes the receipt of any process programs, the material to be processed, and machine-specific calibration. paragraphs.context: 2023 semr 8.3 Data Types 8.3.1 Equipment constants have various uses in HSEM, including the following: Equipment offsets that match the performance of several pieces of equipment that would otherwise perform differently due to inherent manufacturing differences. Examples are home values and motion axis scaling factors. Setting the configuration of the equipment to allow for different material specifications, equipment options, material flows, frequency of automatic functions, etc. Examples are media and yield check frequency. Managing optional machine features. Examples are constants that indicate whether optional features, such as automated media slackers, are present and control the configuration and function of these optional subsystems when they are present. 8.3.2 Status Variables are valid at all times. A status variable may not be changed by the host but may be changed by the equipment or operator. The value of status variables may be queried by the host at any time. 8.3.3 Variables that are valid upon the occurrence of specific collection event can be queried by the host. An attempt to read a data variable at the wrong time shall not generate an error, but the data reported may not have relevant meaning. 8.3.4 Data Item Requirements for Multi-Head, Multi-Site Equipment- The identification for multi-head and multi site data (data variable, status variables, events, etc.) is addressed in this Specification through the use of status variables with list structures. Table 9 Required Variables at Load Complete Variable Name ProcessSiteLoaded ProcessSiteStatus ProcessS i teCount Notes Site loaded status List of sites that are available Total process site count 1 1 SEMI E123-0217 (Reapproved 0623) SEMI 2003, 2023 9 Process Program Management 9.1 Process Program Requirements semr 9.1 . 1 The HSEM requires that the capability of process program management be fully supported for this class of equipment. The HSEM also requires that the process program have a structure that enables the user to build process programs with default conditions that can be overridden for a run. The concepts of process program structure and process program variable parameters are discussed in the following sections. The HSEM also requires the following: Minimum, maximum, and default parameter values must be defined for all process programs. Verification will occur when a process program is downloaded to the equipment; the program syntax must be verified by the equipment manufacturer. Parameter validation will occur when a process program is downloaded. Parameters must be type and range checked. Equipment should provide the functionality to manually or interactively modify the parameters set in the process program. An error message must be generated from the handler if the process program parameters are outside the range of the machine calibration. 9.2 Process Program Structure 9.2.1 A handler process program must

An error message must be generated from the handler if the process program parameters are outside the range of the machine calibration. 9.2 Process Program Structure 9.2.1 A handler process program must contain the following information: Machine-specific configuration parameters, Process-Site-specific information section, Media-Type-specific information section, and Unit (Unit/Package) information section. 9.2.2 When combined, this information constitutes a complete process program. It is emphasized that the HSEM does not enforce the exact format and data type of each section. However, it does provide direction as to what each section should consist of. 9 .2.3 Machine-Specific Configuration Parameters - Each brand or type of handler may have one or more machine specific configuration parameters. Examples of such parameters would be input configuration, number of process sites, and output configuration. Even though they are supplier-specific, these parameters nevertheless play a vital role in the overall generation or creation of a process program. Since the machine-specific parameters can differ from one equipment manufacturer to another, the HSEM does not specify the exact number, data types, and format of these parameters. These details are left to the sole discretion of the equipment manufacturer. 9.2.3.1 In addition, handler process program must support user defined mapping of logical test bin categories that are specific to test recipes and test equipment to handler sort categories. paragraphs.context: required by HSEM are: Establish Communications, Dynamic Event Report Configuration, Variable Data Collection, Status Data Collection, Alarm Management, Remote Control, Equipment Constants, Process Program Management, SEMI E123-0217 (Reapproved 0623) SEMI 2003, 2023 semr Equipment Terminal Services, Clock, Spooling, and Control (Host-Initiated). 12 Alarm Definition 12.1 SEMI E30 has a very restrictive definition of alarms. This Standard requires a broader definition. 1 2.2 Exceptions are classified into two categories: errors and alarms. Alarms may be divided into categories as follows: I . Personal Safety-Condition may be dangerous to people. 2. Equipment Safety- Condition may harm equipment. 3. Parameter Control Warning-Parameter variation outside of preset limits - may harm product. 4. Parameter Control Error - Parameter variation outside of reasonable control limits - may indicate an equipment malfunction. 5. Irrecoverable Error - Intervention required before normal use of equipment can resume. 6. Equipment Status Warning-An unexpected condition has occurred, but operation can continue. 7. Attention Flags - A signal from a process program indicating that a particular step has been reached. 8. Data Integrity-A condition which may cause loss of data; usually related to Stream 6. 12.3 Some alarm conditions may cause more than one type of alann to be issued. For example, a parameter control error on over temperature may also trip a protective device that makes the alarm irrecoverable without some intervention. SEMI E30 constrains the definition of an alarm to be only categories I to 3. However this definition does not account for the modular and parallel nature of

paragraphs.context: example, a parameter control error on over temperature may also trip a protective device that makes the alarm irrecoverable without some intervention. SEMI E30 constrains the definition of an alarm to be only categories I to 3. However this definition does not account for the modular and parallel nature of modern processing equipment. 1 2.4 Relationship to ALARM_PAUSED Process State 1 2.4. 1 The equipment manufacturer should define the relationship between an exception condition and the ALARM_PAUSED process state on a case-by-case basis along these guidelines. I . An exception is not always an alarm. 2. An exception does not necessarily require a process state change to ALARM_PAUSED even though it may ultimately prove to be the root cause of that transition at a later time. 3. Exceptions that force immediate processing stoppage for the primary function of the tool should trigger a transition to ALARM PAUSED. 4. Exceptions reported with an alann code with a category of 1 -3 and 5 as defined above should cause a transition to ALARM PA USED. 5. A machine with currently set alarms may PAUSE instead of transitioning to ALARM_PAUSED ifnone of the currently set exception conditions were the immediate trigger for the transition. 1 3 Related Documents I 3. paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E123.1-0703 (Reapproved 0623) SPECIFICATION FOR SECS-II PROTOCOL FOR HANDLER SPECIFIC EQUIPMENT MODEL (HSEM) This Standard was technically approved by the Information & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on December 21, 2022. Available at www.semiviews.org and www.semi.org in October 2009; originally published July 2003; previously published November 2009. 1 Purpose 1.1 This Standard maps the services and data of SEMl E 123 to SECS-II streams and functions, and data definitions. 2 Scope 2.1 This is a Specification covering equipment supporting automated communication of the handler equipment. 2.2 This Standard applies to all implementations of SEMI E 123 that use the SECS-II message protocol (SEMI E5). Compliance to this Standard requires compliance to both SEMI E 123 and SEMI E5. OTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices, and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 This Specification applies to semiconductor equipment that also use SEMl E30 GEM Standard. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines SEMI E5 - Specification for SEMI Equipment Communications Standard 2 Message Content (SECS-II) SEMI E30 - Specification for the Generic Model for Communications and Control of Manufacturing Equipment (GEM) SEMI El23 - Specification for Handler Equipment Specific Equipment Model (HSEM) OTICE: Unless otherwise indicated,

The following minimum information is required: Class: <ECV, SV, or DVV AL> Format: <SML> <variable name> Description: <If class = DVV AL, description must contain statement of when data is valid.> <If format= ASCII, then a length is required. It is assumed to be left-justified unless otherwise noted.> 6.3 Data Types 6.3.1 Equipment Constants (ECVs) can be changed by the host using S2F 1 5 (New Equipment Constant Send). The operator may be able to change some values, but the equipment does not change the values on its own. The value of an equipment constant may be queried by the host at any time, using the S2Fl3/14 transaction. They reside in nonvolatile memory of the equipment. Equipment constants remain in effect until they are overwritten either by manual entry or by a S2F 1 5. 6.3.2 Status Variables (SVs) are valid at all times. An SV may not be changed by the host but may be changed by the equipment or operator. The value of status variables may be queried by the host at any time using the SI F3/4 or S6F 1 9/20 transactions. 6.3.3 DVV ALs are variables that are valid only upon the occurrence of specific collection events. An attempt to read a variable item at the wrong time does not generate an error, but the data reported may not have relevant meaning. 6.4 For multiple sites scenarios, if

This Standard requires the broader SEMI E5 definition. 7 .1.2 Messages S5F I through S5F8 of this section provide basic alarm messages. The messages S5F9 through S5F 18 provide extended capabilities for Exception Handling. When using messages F l through F8, alarms may be divided into categories as follows: Table 5 Alarm Code Mapping Alarm Name ALCD Description Personal Safety I Condition may be dangerous to people. Equipment Safety Condition may harm equipment. Parameter Control Warning Parameter variation outside of preset limits -may harm product. Parameter Control Error Parameter variation outside of reasonable control limits -may indicate an equipment malfunction. Irrecoverable Error Intervention required before normal use of equipment can resume. Equipment Status Warning An unexpected condition has occurred, but operation can continue. Attention Flags A signal from a process program indicating that a particular step has been reached. Data Integrity A condition that may cause loss of data; usually related to Stream 6. 7.1.3 For messages Fl through F8, it will be the equipment's responsibility to categorize the alarm. Some alarm conditions may cause more than one type of alarm to be issued. For example, a parameter control error on over temperature may also trip a protective device that makes the alarm irrecoverable without some intervention. Exceptions reported with an ALCD with a category of I through 3 and 5 should cause a transition to ALARM PAUSED. 7.2 Recoverable Exceptions 7 .2.1 Any exception that may be programmatically recoverable should

paragraphs.context: of the variables. events, exceptions, and physical equipment configuration available from their equipment. With this information available for conswnption by software systems, it can be used as a tool to aid the process of integrating equipment into a factory's automation system. 2 Scope 2.1 In-Scope 2.1.1 This Standard specifies the classes that suppliers are to use to describe essential data, events, and exceptions provided by their equipment. This Specification only describes information that is static in nature (i.e., information that does not change dynamically while the equipment is running). This Standard also specifies an interface that Clients can use to access this infonnation. 2.1.2 This Specification applies to all semiconductor manufacturing equipment that supports the data acquisition interface defined in the SEMJ Specification for Data Collection Management. 2.2 Ow of Scope 2.2.1 This Specification does not define any new behavior required of the equipment other than that necessary for retrieving information describing equipment configuration. interfaces, and available data, and keeping this information current. 2.2.2 The details of any underlying concepts and behavioral models (e.g., carrier management, process/control job, etc.) that can be described by metadata are to be separately specified in a document dedicated to those concepts. Only the ability to describe the fact that a supplier bas implemented such a concept and that a Client can discover this implementation and any data it can produce is in scope for this Specification. 2.2.3 This Specification does not require that the metadata provided by the equipment be directly human readable. Adjunct specifications shall be developed to bind this Specification to an implementation technology. 3.2 Other limitations 3.2.1 This Specification does not define the mechanism used for determining how to locate EDA Applications that are to receive notifications defined by this Specification. It assumes that the equipment has established a communications context with any EDA Applications that can submit requests. and has provided any context necessary to send notifications to EDA Applications. 3.2.2 This Specification does not define the mechanism used for identifying or authenticating Clients. Fire-and-Forget messages are messages that involve a sender and a receiver. paragraphs.context: specific implementation technology. 9.4.2 If the Subordinate Standard specifies the data and time format as a string data type. the date and time information shall be formaned according to ISO 860 I. 9.4.2.1 ISO 8601 permits a variety of possible formats for the same time value, so it is necessary to establish a convention for the format used by time values in this Standard. 9.4.2.2 For representing time values, this Standard restricts the string representation of time to the form CCYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss.fffffffff [-+)hh:mm. 'CC' is the century (first century starts at '00'), 'YY' is the year, 'MM' is the month, and 'DD' is the day. each represented as a two-digit number with leading zeroes as needed. For years beyond 9999, additional digits can be added to the 'CC' field. 'T' separates date information from time SEMI E125-1225 SEMI 2003, 2025 semr information. 'hh' is the hour, 'mm' are the minutes, 'ss' are the seconds, and 'fffffffff' are the sub-seconds, supporting up to one billionth of a second (nanosecond) precision in local time. If equipment can't measure time with full precision, it shall fill in the sub-second field with zeroes as appropriate up to the measurable precision (e.g., if time can be measured with centi-second precision, all time values would contain zeros in the third through ninth columns of the sub-second field). '[-+]hh:mm' describes the difference from Coordinated Universal Time expressed as either +hh:mm or -hh:mm. 10 Interfaces for Managing Equipment Metadata I 0.1 Equipment Metadara Manager I 0.1.1 Figure 5 shows

A constraint is documented as a propositional formula, which evaluates TRUE with valid parameter values. Relational algebra defines a rich syntax for propositional formulas; however, this syntax is not commonly used for commercial applications. Standard Query Language (SQL) is a well-documented commercial application of relational algebra well suited to this application. Specifically, the syntax of the WHERE clause (of the SQL ELE T statement) is suitable for describing constraints on Parameter values. The WHERE clause of the SQL ELECT statement is a propositional-expression that consists of one or more comparisons of values with constants. It has the following form. WHERE propositional-expression (nested-propositional-expression]; SEMI E1 25-1225 SEMI 2003, 2025 semr For the purposes of this Specification, a subset of the SQL WHERE syntax (the complete syntax is discussed in RELATED I FORMATIO 2, R2-1) shall be used to define constraints. Events generated by the equipment shall either be defined within the context of these state machine descriptions or defined as a SimpleEvent associated with an equipment node (refer to 11.3), even if the described state machine has not been literally implemented by the supplier. Further, this Specification does not require the supplier to provide a literal implementation of the state machines described by these classes. 11.6.1.2 A StateMachine that defines behavior that is implemented by many equipment components, for example, need only be defined once. Each component that implements the behavior described by that StateMachine can then refer to the events it defines through the use of the StateMachinelnstance class (refer to 11.6.8) and StateMachineReference class. It is not necessary to define multiple StateMachines to accommodate multiple components that each exhibit the same behavior and generate the same events. For example, if the equipment being described has four pump components that exhibit the same behavior, have the same underlying StateMachine, and generate the same set of events, only one StateMachine description is necessary. Each pump component can then act as a different source of the same set of events defined for that StateMachine, each pump executing the StateMachine independently of the other pumps. 11.6.2 StateMachine - This class represents a collection of states and transitions that together comprise a finite state machine. paragraphs.context: state machine as part of an equipment node in the metadata. A state machine reference is representative of all of the state machine's instances, thereby allowing state machine event data collection for any instance of the state machine even when the instances are created and deleted dynamically. This class also provides a way to describe parameters that are available for data collection with the state machine events in the StateMachineReference. The StateMachineReference itself remains persistent by the equipment node even when there are no instances at a specific time. 11.6.6.2 As an example, suppose that a state machine represents a substrate state model. Multiple substrate objects are continuously created as the material arrives for processing and deleted when the material is removed after processing. A StateMachinelnstance cannot be used to represent the substrate's state model because the StateMachinelnstance is static in the model and can represent only a single substrate. When a state machine event from a StateMachineReference is referenced for event data collection. then the state machine event is reported whenever the transition occurs for any substrate. The identity of the substrate is published in the stateMachinelnstanceParameter. TI1e state machine event from the StateMachineReference can be referenced even when no substrates exist. The fingerprint can be used to quickly check if the equipment metadata is the same without obtaining all the equipment metadata and comparing each metadata component explicitly. 'OTE I: Possible use case scenarios include an equipment user that wants lo sec if equipment mctadata changed after a maintenance activity on the equipment, or wants to know if two pieces of equipment from the same equipment supplier have the same equipment mctadata so they can re-use data collection plans or reports without any customiz.ations. 1 1 .9. 1.1 How the equipment calculates this fingerprint value is an implementation detail. Some equipment may hash their internal metadata configuration object. Other equipment may create a revision identifier based on internal attributes. 1 1.9.1.2 Equipment from the same equipment supplier that have the same equipment metadata shall have the same fingerprint value.

SEMI E 125 infonnation is proprietary to the equipment supplier, and should be protected against unauthorized access within the factory. 7.2.1.2 For this reason, all operations defined by the SEMI El25 EquipmentMetadataManager interface can be exchanged using the HTTPS transport (HTTP 1.1 over SSL 3.0) by enabling SSL authentication as specified in SEMI E 132.1. Once enabled via SEMI EI 32.1, the equipment shall require mutual client-server SSL authentication for all SEMI E 125 operations supported by the equipment. and the equipment shall not provide access to any operations except via the HTTPS transport. 7.2.2 There may be some integration, development, and test environments in which it is not practical or feasible to install equipment and client certificates in order to communicate with the equipment. paragraphs.context: integration, development, and test environments in which it is not practical or feasible to install equipment and client certificates in order to communicate with the equipment. As a practical consideration for such environments only, the equipment shall support the ability of the user to disable SSL authentication for the SEMI E 125 EquipmentMetadataManager interface, using the equipment configuration specified by SEMI E 132.1. Users should be aware that disabling SSL authentication opens up complete unrestricted access to SEMI E 125 data for any application with network access to the equipment. 7.2.2.1 Regardless of whether or not SSL authentication is enabled, the equipment shall reject all requests from clients that do not provide a valid SEMI El28 session identifier in the SOAP envelope header provided with the request. The SEMI El 28 session identifier shall be provided via the SEMI E 128 SyocHeader element, as specified by SEMI E 128. If a client does not provide a recognized session identifier, the equipment shall immediately reject the request using the SEM1 Common Error data type, with an error code of '6005' and a source of 'um:semi-org:E 132' as specified in SEMI El32.I. The equipment shall perform no other processing of a SEMI El25 request if the request is found to include an invalid SEMI El32 session ident.ifier. or if no session identifier is provided. Referenceable Parameters are defined either by SEMIObjTypes as 'Attributes' or 'EventData', or by equipment nodes in the set of Parameters provided with 'EquipmentNodeDescription' metadata. Equipment nodes and SEMIObjTypes are said to be valid Parameter 'sources'. Parameters defined for Exceptions are not referenceable. This type defines two attributes, 'source and 'name' that are used to uniquely identify Parameters in equipment metadata. SEMI E125.1-0414 SEMI 2005, 2014 semr 7.2.11.4.5.1 Parameter references may be 'absolute' or 'relative'. An absolute Parameter reference is used when referring to a Parameter that is defined by a different source than the one making the reference. A relative reference is used when referring to a Parameter that is defined by the same source as the one making the reference. It is expected that the most common Parameter references in equipment metadata will be relative references. 7.2.11.4.5. l. l For an example of an 'absolute' reference, consider two equipment nodes: a 'TemperatureController' equipment node that provides a 'TemperatureSetpoint' Parameter; and a TemperatureSensor' equipment node that provides a 'MeasuredTemperature' Parameter. paragraphs.context: on a per-Session basis. For example, the same SEMI El32 Principal may establish one or more SEMI El32 Sessions on the equipment. If only one of those Sessions requests revision notification, the equipment shall only enable notifications for that specific session. The equipment shall not notify other Sessions that have not enabled notification. As described in 7.3.1.2, the equipment shall use the URL provided with the HTTPEndpoint information provided with the SEMJ E 132 EstablishSession request to send all SEMI E 125 notifications. 7.2. 16.3 SEMI E 125 specifies that the equipment shall disable notifications if it detects that a client is no longer reachable. Clients (SEMI El32 Principals) are considered reachable if and only if their SEMI El32 Session has not been closed (i.e., the Session is in either the 'Established' or 'Frozen' state). as defined by the SEMI E 132 Session State Model. If the equipment or the client terminates a SEMI El32 Session for any reason (causes the Session to close), notifications for that Session shall be automatically disabled by the equipment. That client will be required to re-establish a SEMI E 132 Session and re-send a NotifyOnRevisions request in order to re-establish revision notifications. If the client's Session is placed in the 'Frozen' state, the equipment shall retain that Session's notification settings in non-volatile memory, and resume notifications when the Session returns to the 'Established' state. 7.2.16.4 WSDL Operation Binding 7.2.16.4.1 Refer to the operation binding for ' otifyOnRevisions' in the binding wsdl file. It is possible for the Client to reconnect to the Equipment Server later on and continue to do management activities. 9.8 Session Identifier 9.8.1 The session identifier (SessionJd) is defined in SEMI El 32 and is generated by the equipment during the SEMI E 1 32 EstablishSession operation. It is a universally unique identifier (UUlD) that is guaranteed unique across space and time (refer to RFC 4122 for specific details on syntax and fonnat of a UUlD). 9.8.2 In Protocol Buffers messages, the Sessionld is represented as a string data type, as it is more easily understood in its human-readable form. 9.8.3 The session identifier is specified in all requests to the Equipment Server over a SEMI El32 authenticated session. gRPC's metadata infrastructure is used to pass the SessionJd for gRPC methods. This provides the most transparent way of sending the session identifier without affecting the input/output parameters of the individual unary methods. gRPC metadata takes name-value pairs and stores them in the HTTP/2 header data. NOTE 4: For more information about gRPC's mctadata infrastructure, refer to SEMI El79, Related Information 2. paragraphs.context: on May 5. 2022. R1-1 Background Information RI- I.I EDA Freeze Version 2 used HTTP/I.I with SOAP/XML as the mechanism to transmit information between EDA clients and EDA servers. This section describes how that functionality can be mapped to gRPC and Protocol Buffers technology. R 1-1.2 It is not as efficient to structure Protocol Buffers messages exactly the same as the original SOAP/XML messages defined in SEMJ El25.I. It does not leverage the strengths of the new technology (e.g., oneoffields) and there are workarounds put into the XML schema to deal with the string representation limitations within SOAP/XML that no longer make sense (e.g., sending additional metadata and additional layers ofinfonnation). R1-2 Message Structures Rl-2.1 With SEMI El 25.1, Sessionld, Client, and Equipment information were encoded in the SOAP Header via the SyncHeader and SyncHashHeader information per SEMI E 128. (SyncHeader has a From. To, and Sessionld elements. SyncHashHeader has a From, To, and SessionldHashed elements). This meant that every message sent to and from the EDA server had access to this information. gRPC and Protocol Buffers do not use SOAP Headers so a new infrastructure needs to be used. Equipment information and Clientld in the headers was never used and has been removed in this Specification. The Sessionld information will be specified for gRPC methods via the gRPC metadata infrastructure. R1-3 Wrapper Messages Rl -3.1 For efficiencies, wrapper messages with just a single piece of information were removed when creating equivalent Protocol Buffers messages. The parent message will include

R1-3 Wrapper Messages Rl -3.1 For efficiencies, wrapper messages with just a single piece of information were removed when creating equivalent Protocol Buffers messages. The parent message will include that piece of infonnation directly. Rl-3.1.1 For example, in SEMJ El25. I there was a TransitionReferenceType object defined that had a single attribute 'transitionld' that represented the transitions. There is no corresponding Protocol Buffers message for TransitionReferenceType. The parent message (StateMachineEventType) will directly reference the transition identifiers. R 1-3.2 The exception to this approach is the input and output stream parameter messages used in gRPC method calls. gRPC streaming methods will have both a request stream message and response stream message parameter defined (even if they are empty messages), as this will future proof the method definition. If the RPC method needs to have input parameters in the future, they can be added to the input stream message. mtl1er than redefining the RPC method. R1-4 Error Handling Rl-4.1 Many XML method calls that returned information to the calling code had a choice between the service result (if the method call was successf ul) and an Error result (if the method call was not successful). This was handled by an xsd:choice in the schema file. Method results could have multiple elements. This makes using Protocol Buffers' oneof structure difficult. paragraphs.context: was successf ul) and an Error result (if the method call was not successful). This was handled by an xsd:choice in the schema file. Method results could have multiple elements. This makes using Protocol Buffers' oneof structure difficult. R 1-4.2 With Protocol Buffers messages, error information is always specified in the Protocol Buffers message as an oneof structure. If the operation was successful, the implementer should not assign anything to the Errorlnfonnation structure. Protocol Buffers syntax does not allow oneofstructures to be embedded in another oneofstructure. All the fields in an oneof structure share the same memory, and at most one field can be set at the same time. SEMI E125.2-1225 SEMI 2022, 2025 semr Setting any member of the oneof fields automatically clears all the other members. You can check which value in an oneof field is specified (if any) using a special case() or WhichOneof{) method, depending on the target development language. If there is nothing assigned to the Errorlnfonnation field, the case() or WhichOneof{) method will specify None. This can be used to detect if the operation was successful. If the operation was not successful (something is assigned to the Error field), the other message parameters that normally contain data will not be valid (e.g., ResultsDara field). R l -4.3 All methods that return information to the caller should have Error information defined in the response message. This allows the Error information to be extended if additional errors are required in the future, and back\vard compatibility issues

R2-2 Communicating over Secure Communications R2-2. l The software application who initiates the gRPC connection can specify if that connection is using secure communications (refer to SEMI E 132.2). For example: When the Client establishes a gRPC connection to the Equipment Server, they connect on a different port for secure communications. As part of the SEMI El32 EstablishSession, if the endpoint infonnation is provided to have the Equipment Server open the connection to the Consumer, the equipment user specifies if it is a secure connection. R2-2.2 There can be a performance cost to using secure communications, so the equipment user may not wish to send all their data over secure communications. qas.question: Who is not held responsible for identifying patented technology or copyrighted items?

answers.text: SEMJ shaJI

qas.question: What position does SEMI take regarding the validity of patent rights or copyrights?

answers.text: SEMI takes no position respecting the validity of any patent rights or copyrights asserted in connection with any item mentioned in this Standard or Safety Guideline.

qas.question: Whose responsibility is the determination of any such patent rights or copyrights and the risk of infringement?

answers.text: entirely their own responsibility.

qas.question: What is forbidden without express permission?

answers.text: Reproduction of the contents in whole or in part
